layers.name: Marcus Orchestration
layers.description: The Marcus-SPOC orchestrator, conversational CLI surface, lesson-plan compilation, and UDAC / enrichment-consumption wiring that drives an instance of the production runtime.

layers.name: Specialist Agents & Composers
layers.description: Per-specialist LangGraph agents (irene, gary, texas, enrique, vision, wanda, kira, motion_planner, quinn_r, and _shared audits) and the composer nodes that assemble their outputs into decks, motion, and workbook artifacts.

layers.name: Gates & Workflow Substrate
layers.description: HIL gate decision modules, the pipeline-manifest compiler, Cora block-mode dev-graph enforcement, and ledger/manifest substrate that gate and sequence the production walk.

layers.name: Domain Models
layers.description: Pydantic v2 domain and runtime-state models plus emitted JSON schemas under app/models that define decision-cards, lesson-plan envelopes, contributions side-cars, and the shared data contracts every specialist reads and writes.

layers.name: Runtime & Transport
layers.description: Runtime execution and persistence concerns: Postgres checkpointer/retention, resume-and-recover replay, HTTP transport, MCP server, parity checks, and audit plumbing that carry state through the graph.

layers.name: Operator & Developer Tooling
layers.description: Standalone scripts under scripts/ (BaseAPIClient and concrete API clients, content generators, analysis/acoustics diagnostics, operator utilities) plus scratchpad render/trial drivers used for live proofing and off-the-books discovery runs.

layers.name: BMAD Agent Skills
layers.description: Operator-facing BMAD agent skill packages under skills/ (SKILL.md persona files, reference contracts, and helper scripts) for the custom production personas Marcus routes work to.

layers.name: Project Root & Configuration
layers.description: Top-level project configuration and metadata: environment templates, git/coverage attributes, and tooling artifacts that sit at the repository root.

tour.title: Runtime Platform Overview
tour.description: Begin with the runtime package README to understand the LangGraph execution substrate that underpins every production trial. This document explains how checkpointers, thread retention, and cleanup policies keep long-running orchestration durable and reproducible. It frames the platform as a deterministic multi-agent engine rather than a loose collection of chat scripts.

tour.title: Marcus-SPOC CLI Entry
tour.description: The Marcus CLI `__main__` module is the operator-facing entry surface for the entire production runtime. It routes subcommands for trials, gate verdicts, and ad-hoc tooling, making it the first code path an operator hits when launching work. From here the dependency graph fans out into the conversational SPOC layer and the production runner you will explore next.

tour.title: Marcus-SPOC Conversational Layer
tour.description: Building on the CLI entry point, `marcus_spoc.py` implements the conversational single-point-of-contact that sits over the deterministic production engine. It narrates kickoff beats, picker recommendations, and research results while a guard ensures the chatting model never drives engine state directly. This separation is the core Marcus-SPOC product pattern: friendly operator dialogue backed by a frozen, manifest-compiled graph.
tour.languageLesson: The SPOC pattern uses a conversational LLM layer strictly for narration and intake, while all state mutations flow through the compiled LangGraph runner — preventing chat drift from altering production invariants.

tour.title: Trial Launch CLI
tour.description: The trial CLI shim is the concrete command path operators use to start a production-clone run with a corpus input and preset configuration. It bridges the conversational SPOC surface to the orchestration layer, wiring cascade config, gate resume APIs, and the production runner. Understanding this file clarifies how an operator action becomes a persisted LangGraph thread.

tour.title: Production Graph Runner
tour.description: At the heart of orchestration, `production_runner.py` composes the full production graph for migrated trials — the highest fan-out node in the codebase. It dispatches specialists with retry logic, applies gate verdicts to RunState, and coordinates research, enrichment, and publishing legs. Every specialist graph you will meet later is invoked through this runner's dispatch and envelope contracts.

tour.title: Manifest Compiler
tour.description: Before the runner executes anything, the manifest compiler transforms a declarative `PipelineManifest` into a concrete LangGraph `StateGraph`. It resolves specialist dispatch nodes, validates production gate codes, and loads the dispatch registry so the graph topology is frozen at compile time. The companion README documents the `app.manifest` package's role in keeping pipeline structure auditable and reproducible.
tour.languageLesson: Manifest compilation separates graph topology (declared in YAML/JSON manifests) from runtime execution (LangGraph StateGraph). Changing pipeline structure requires recompilation, preserving frozen-graph reproducibility.

tour.title: RunState and Reproducibility
tour.description: RunState is the top-level persisted state object — the second-highest fan-in node in the graph — encoding five reproducibility invariants (NFR-X1 through X5). The `RunState` class and its module define how trials track completion, variant selections, operator verdicts, and sanctum fingerprints across the entire run lifecycle. The state README explains the validator layer that enforces these invariants at persistence boundaries.
tour.languageLesson: Pydantic v2 models with explicit validators enforce reproducibility invariants at the schema boundary — RunState is both the LangGraph state container and the audit record for trial integrity.

tour.title: Specialist State Model
tour.description: While RunState tracks the overall trial, SpecialistState captures per-specialist persistence including conversation artifacts, model-resolution audit entries, and cache-prefix hashes. The JSON schema formalizes the shape that every specialist graph reads and writes through the scaffold contract. This model is the shared vocabulary between the production runner's dispatch layer and each specialist's nine-node subgraph.

tour.title: Nine-Node Specialist Scaffold
tour.description: Every production specialist conforms to the canonical nine-node scaffold defined in `_scaffold/contract.py` and `_scaffold/graph.py`. The contract discovers specialist IDs, validates scaffold conformance, and builds specialist graphs; the reference graph implements the receive→plan→act→verify→reflect→gate cycle shared across all agents. The scaffold expertise README is the template operators and developers use when onboarding a new specialist persona.
tour.languageLesson: LangGraph specialist subgraphs follow a fixed nine-node topology (receive, plan, act, verify, reflect, gate decision, emit spans, etc.). The scaffold contract enforces this shape so the manifest compiler can wire any specialist uniformly.

tour.title: Irene — Lesson Authoring
tour.description: Irene's graph is the Pass 2 lesson-authoring specialist — a scaffold-conformant LangGraph with the highest specialist fan-out in the codebase. It transforms blueprint and coverage inputs into narrated lesson content with pedagogical annotations. Study this graph to see how the nine-node scaffold handles multi-pass authoring, voice-direction annotation, and fidelity checks in a real production specialist.

tour.title: Gary — Visual Generation
tour.description: Gary's graph implements the gamma-generation specialist responsible for slide content, diagram cards, and fidelity slides dispatched through the production runner's writer layer. It integrates with external visual generation services while maintaining payload contracts and learned dependency manifests. This specialist illustrates how the scaffold's `_act` node orchestrates external API dispatch with tamper-evident receipts.

tour.title: Texas — Research Retrieval
tour.description: Texas is the source-wrangler specialist that fulfills research dispatch intents from the orchestrator's citation and retrieval wiring. Its graph loads directive bundles, enforces word-floor expectations, and produces grounded retrieval outputs that feed the research-citation leg of the braid arc. Texas demonstrates how a specialist encapsulates a Shape-3-Disciplined retrieval contract behind the standard scaffold interface.

tour.title: Enrique and Workbook Producer
tour.description: Enrique handles ElevenLabs voice-generation dispatch, converting authored narration into audio assets through its scaffold-conformant graph and payload contracts. The workbook producer specialist composes companion learner workbooks (Markdown to DOCX) as a separate graph in the composition catalog. Together they show how parallel specialist subgraphs extend the core lesson pipeline into multimedia and learner-facing deliverables.

tour.title: Gates, Verdicts, and HIL
tour.description: Production gates G1 through G4 pause the graph at critical decision points, surfacing DecisionCard verdicts through the resume API and verdict module. The HIL intake callable bridges operator pre-collected decisions into the graph, while party-mode review appears as a LangGraph interrupt for multi-agent consensus gates. Gate error types enforce that verdict resume invariants cannot be bypassed — completing the operator-trust loop from Marcus-SPOC intake through frozen-graph execution.
tour.languageLesson: LangGraph interrupts pause graph execution at gate nodes until an operator submits a DecisionCard verdict via the resume API. HIL gates are first-class graph edges, not post-hoc callbacks — ensuring every human decision is captured in RunState.
